evals.name: product-listing-page
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for a product listing page. The page has a site header with logo and main navigation, a search bar, a sidebar with filter options (price range checkboxes, category checkboxes, an 'in stock only' toggle), and a main content area showing 6 product cards. Each card has an image, product name, price, a short description, and an 'Add to cart' button. There's also a footer with contact info and secondary nav links. Use realistic product names and prices — not 'Product 1'. The page title is 'Running Shoes — SportGear'.
key_expectations: Uses <header>, <nav>, <main>, <aside>, <footer> landmarks appropriately
<main> contains only the primary page content — site header, footer, and primary nav are outside it
Skip navigation links are the first focusable elements — at minimum 'Skip to content', with 'Skip to search' also appropriate given the search bar
Navigation uses <nav> with a label that does NOT include the word 'navigation'
Search uses the <search> element
Filter sidebar uses <form> with <fieldset>/<legend> for each filter group
Product cards use <article> (they are self-contained)
Product cards are in a <ul> (unordered — the count helps the user, but sequence is not meaningful)
Heading hierarchy flows logically: h1 for page, h2 for sections, h3 for product names
Product card heading level is not hardcoded to h3 — ideally configurable or documented
Buttons are <button> elements, not <div> or <a> with click handlers
'Add to cart' buttons have accessible names that distinguish which product
Price is not marked up as a heading
No ARIA attributes used where native HTML provides the same semantics
Filter checkboxes use proper <label> elements, not placeholders
The 'in stock only' toggle uses a native checkbox or switch pattern, not a custom div

evals.name: faq-page
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for a FAQ page for a SaaS product called 'FlowBoard'. The page should have: a page header with breadcrumb navigation (Home > Support > FAQ), a search/filter input to narrow down questions, 3 FAQ categories ('Getting Started', 'Billing & Plans', 'Integrations') each with 3-4 questions, and a 'Still need help?' section at the bottom with a link to contact support and a phone number. Questions should be expandable. Use realistic questions and short but helpful answers.
key_expectations: Breadcrumb uses <nav> with an aria-label like 'Breadcrumb'
<main> wraps only the FAQ content — site header and footer are outside it
Skip navigation links are the first focusable elements — 'Skip to content' at minimum, 'Skip to search' also appropriate given the search/filter input
Breadcrumb items are in an <ol> (ordered — position matters)
FAQ categories use <h2> headings
Individual questions use <details>/<summary> for progressive disclosure
Questions are NOT marked up as <dl> (these are not term-definition pairs)
The expandable pattern uses native <details>/<summary>, not custom ARIA disclosure
Search/filter input has a visible or visually hidden <label>
'Still need help?' uses an appropriate heading level (h2)
Contact link is an <a> element, phone number is an <a href='tel:...'>
The page has a single <h1>
No heading levels are skipped
Contact info in footer uses <address> only if it's about the site owner/author

evals.name: data-table-with-controls
evals.prompt: Create an HTML page showing employee data in a table. The table has columns: Name, Department, Role, Start Date, and Status (Active/On Leave/Departed). Above the table there's a toolbar with: a search input, a dropdown to filter by department, a dropdown to filter by status, and a 'Download CSV' button. The table should have 8 rows of realistic employee data. Below the table, show 'Showing 8 of 42 employees' and pagination controls (Previous, page numbers 1-5, Next). Include a caption that describes the table.
key_expectations: Uses <table> with <caption>, <thead>, <tbody>
Header cells use <th scope='col'>
Status column content is not conveyed by colour alone
Search input has a <label>
Department and status filters use native <select> elements
Filter controls are grouped in a <form> or <search> element
'Download CSV' is a <button> (or <a> with download attribute if linking to a file)
Pagination uses <nav> with a label
Pagination links/buttons clearly indicate current page (aria-current='page')
Previous/Next are <button> or <a> elements, not divs

'Showing 8 of 42 employees' is a

or status text, not a heading
Table is not wrapped in a role='grid' unless genuinely interactive
No table semantics are stripped by CSS display properties (or if they are, roles restore them)

evals.name: multi-step-form
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for a multi-step checkout form for an online store. Step 1: Shipping information (full name, email, phone, street address, city, postal code, country dropdown). Step 2: Payment method (credit card with card number, expiry, CVV — just the fields, no actual processing). Step 3: Order review showing a summary of 2 items with quantities and a total. Each step should be clearly delineated. Include a progress indicator showing which step the user is on. Include proper error message markup for when a field is invalid, demonstrated on the email field.
key_expectations: Each step uses <fieldset> with <legend> for grouping
All inputs have associated <label> elements (not placeholder-only)
Email error uses aria-invalid='true' and aria-describedby pointing to error message
Error message text is actionable (states problem AND guides fix)
Country uses native <select>
Progress indicator conveys current step accessibly (not just visually)
Card number, expiry, CVV fields have appropriate autocomplete attributes
Phone field has type='tel'
Email field has type='email'
Order summary in step 3 could use a <table> or <dl> — either is acceptable
Submit/continue buttons are <button> elements with clear labels
No placeholder text used as a substitute for labels
Steps have heading structure that fits the page (not all h1 or all h2)
Required fields are indicated (not just with colour)

evals.name: blog-article-page
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for a blog article page. The article is titled 'Why We Migrated From REST to GraphQL' by 'Priya Sharma', published on '2025-11-12'. The page has: site header with logo and nav, the article itself with an intro paragraph, 3 sections with h2 headings ('The Problem', 'Our Approach', 'Results'), a code block in the second section, an aside with a pull quote from the article, author bio at the bottom of the article, a 'Related Articles' section showing 3 article cards, a comments section with 2 existing comments (each with author name, date, and comment text), and a 'Leave a comment' form (name, email, comment textarea). Use realistic content — don't use lorem ipsum.
key_expectations: The article uses the <article> element
<main> wraps the article, related articles, and comments — site header and footer are outside it
Skip navigation links are the first focusable elements — 'Skip to content' is sufficient for this page layout
Article date uses <time> with a datetime attribute
Article sections use headings (h2) within the article
Pull quote uses <aside> (tangentially related content)
The pull quote does NOT use <blockquote> unless it's genuinely a quote from another source
Code block uses <pre><code>
Author bio could use <footer> within the <article> (contact info about the author)
Related articles section uses a heading (h2) and article cards in a list
Each related article card uses <article>
Comments are each an <article> (self-contained, could be syndicated)
Comment form uses <fieldset>/<legend> or at minimum proper <label> elements
Comment author name and date use appropriate elements (<strong>/<b> or <span> for name, <time> for date)
The <address> element is used correctly if present (only for contact info about the author/owner)
Heading hierarchy is consistent: h1 for article title, h2 for sections, h2 for 'Related Articles', h2 for 'Comments'
No heading levels are skipped

evals.name: dashboard-sidebar-nav
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for a dashboard layout. It has: a fixed sidebar with the app logo, a primary navigation (Dashboard, Projects, Team, Reports, Settings — where 'Projects' is the current page), a secondary navigation section at the bottom of the sidebar ('Help & Support', 'What's New', user avatar with name 'Alex Chen' and a dropdown menu with Profile, Preferences, Sign Out). The main content area has a page heading 'Projects', a description paragraph, and 3 project cards each with a project name, status badge (Active/Paused/Complete), last updated date, and member count. Don't write any CSS — just the HTML structure.
key_expectations: Sidebar navigation uses <nav> with a descriptive label
<main> wraps only the projects content area — the sidebar navigation is outside it
Skip navigation links are the first focusable elements — 'Skip to content' at minimum, with 'Skip to navigation' also appropriate since the sidebar nav is not at the top of the DOM
If there are two <nav> elements, each has a distinct label
Nav items are in a <ul> list
Current page ('Projects') is indicated with aria-current='page'
User dropdown menu uses a <button> with the Popover API — not <details>/<summary> (which is for content disclosure, not action menus) and not a custom ARIA-heavy solution
Dropdown menu items: 'Profile' and 'Preferences' are links, 'Sign Out' is a button or form
Main content area uses <main>
Project cards are in a list
Status badges are not conveyed by colour alone
Dates use <time> with datetime attribute
'Projects' page heading is <h1>
Project card names are appropriate heading level (h2)
The secondary nav section has a label distinguishing it from the primary nav
Logo is either a link to home or an appropriate element, with alt text if an image

evals.name: pricing-comparison-table
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for a pricing page with 3 plan tiers: 'Starter' ($9/mo), 'Professional' ($29/mo), and 'Enterprise' (custom pricing). Show a comparison table of features across plans. Features: 'Projects' (5 / Unlimited / Unlimited), 'Storage' (10GB / 100GB / Unlimited), 'Team members' (1 / 10 / Unlimited), 'API access' (No / Yes / Yes), 'Priority support' (No / No / Yes), 'Custom integrations' (No / No / Yes). Each plan also has a CTA button ('Start Free Trial' for first two, 'Contact Sales' for Enterprise). The Professional plan should be highlighted as 'Most Popular'. Include a heading and a brief intro paragraph.
key_expectations: Feature comparison uses a <table> (data has meaningful rows AND columns)
Table has <caption>
Plan names are <th scope='col'> in the header row
Feature names are <th scope='row'> in each row
Yes/No values are not conveyed only by colour or icons — text is present
'Most Popular' badge is accessible (not just visual)
CTA buttons are <button> or <a> elements depending on where they lead
'Contact Sales' is a link if it goes to a contact page, button if it opens a modal
Plan names above the table (if present as cards) use appropriate heading levels
Prices are not headings
The page has one h1
Table structure uses <thead> and <tbody>
Boolean features (API access: Yes/No) don't use checkmark icons without text alternatives

evals.name: settings-form-with-grouped-controls
evals.prompt: Create the HTML for an account settings page. It has sections for: 'Profile' (display name text input, bio textarea, avatar upload), 'Notifications' (email notifications toggle, push notifications toggle, notification frequency radio buttons: Instant/Daily Digest/Weekly Digest), 'Privacy' (profile visibility radio: Public/Friends Only/Private, checkboxes for 'Show online status' and 'Allow search engines to index my profile'), and a 'Danger Zone' section with a 'Delete Account' button that is visually distinct. Each section should be clearly grouped. Include a 'Save Changes' button at the bottom.
key_expectations: Each settings section uses <fieldset> with <legend>
All inputs have <label> elements
Radio button groups are within a <fieldset> with a <legend> describing the group
Toggle switches use checkbox inputs (not custom div patterns)
File upload uses <input type='file'> with a label
Textarea has a label
'Delete Account' is a <button> — not a link
'Save Changes' is a <button type='submit'>
Section headings use consistent heading levels
Legends may serve as visual headings (no need for both legend AND heading for same text)
No placeholder-only inputs
Radio buttons have matching name attributes within their group
The form as a whole is a <form> element
